Proteins that regulate mitochondrial dynamics are integrated into cellular signaling pathways. For instance, the mitochondrial fission factor Drp1 is regulated by various post-translational modifications that interact with cellular processes like apoptosis, Ca 2+ signaling, hypoxia response, and the cell cycle [ 2 ]. Recent evidence indicates that interactions between mitochondria and other organelles significantly contribute to mitochondrial quality control and functional regulation. Thus, the disruption of mitochondrial function can lead to various diseases [ 3 ]. Mitochondria have cell-type-specific phenotypes, perform dozens of interconnected functions, and undergo dynamic and often reversible physiological recalibrations [ 4 ]. In the brain, mitochondria are abundant in neuronal dendrites and synaptic terminals due to the high energy demands of neurons, which cannot store ATP [ 5 ]. Mitochondria can change their shape dynamically through controlled processes of fusion and fission. Mitochondria can also move actively between different parts of the cell, such as the cell body, axon, and presynaptic terminals [ 6 ]. Thus, adapting the energy supply to meet demand and maintain mitochondrial health is essential for cellular homeostasis and proper neuronal function [ 7 ], such as synaptic plasticity [ 8 ] and neurogenesis [ 9 ]. Mitochondria also play a central role in synthesizing biomolecules, including neurotransmitters [ 6 ] and hormones [ 10 ]. Based on these points, mitochondrial activity is crucial for modulating neuronal activity, short- and long-term plasticity, cellular resilience, and behavioral adaptations [ 11 , 12 ]. Mitochondria also interact with the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) at specific sites known as mitochondria-associated ER membranes, which cover 5–20% of the mitochondrial surface. Similar to how mitochondria create a network throughout neurons, the ER also forms a distributed network. Interactions at these contact sites are vital in regulating various physiological processes, including ATP production, autophagy, mitochondrial movement, and apoptosis [ 13 ]. Mitochondria are actively transported to and from synapses to supply energy and buffer Ca 2+ levels, which is critical for sustaining synaptic activity. At presynaptic sites, they provide ATP to support prolonged synaptic function and buffer Ca 2+ signals, thereby modulating neurotransmission and potentially limiting synaptic activity. The dynamic regulation of mitochondrial presence at synapses may also enhance computational flexibility [ 7 ]. Mitochondrial dysfunction leads to ROS synthesis and neuronal damage, which cause brain aging [ 14 ], neurodegenerative diseases [ 15 ], and psychiatric disorders [ 16 ], including bipolar disorder (BD) [ 17 ]. BD is a mental illness marked by extreme mood swings, alternating between depressive and manic episodes. Bipolar I disorder is defined by distinct manic episodes, which often include symptoms such as excessive confidence, grandiosity, increased talkativeness, severe disinhibition, irritability, a reduced need for sleep, and an intensely elevated mood. Psychotic features, such as delusions and hallucinations, are present in up to 75% of manic episodes and often disrupt psychosocial functioning to the point of requiring hospitalization. In contrast, bipolar II disorder is mainly characterized by recurrent depressive episodes alternating with hypomanic episodes instead of full mania [ 18 ]. BD has a global lifetime prevalence of 2.4% and a 12-month prevalence of 1.5% [ 19 ]. Pharmacological treatment, including mood stabilizers like lithium, valproate, and lamotrigine, along with antipsychotic agents, is the primary treatment approach. Clinicians adjust medications based on the patient’s manic, depressive, or remission phases. Some patients may not respond to these pharmacological treatments, potentially resulting in cognitive impairment and social dysfunction [ 20 ]. Despite numerous studies, predicting treatment response and prognosis in BD remains challenging [ 21 , 22 , 23 ]. BD ranks as the 17th leading source of disability among all diseases worldwide [ 24 ]. Approximately 6 to 7% of individuals with BD commit suicide, with studies showing that their suicide rates are 20 to 30 times higher than those in the general population [ 25 ]. Although BD has significant social impacts, its exact causes remain unknown. Mitochondrial dysfunction has been proposed as one possible explanation [ 17 , 26 ]. Evidence supporting this hypothesis includes creatine uptake studies using magnetic resonance spectroscopy in BD [ 27 , 28 ], postmortem brain studies [ 29 , 30 , 31 ], and the frequent comorbidity of BD with mitochondrial diseases [ 32 ]. Numerous reports have supported the involvement of mitochondrial dysfunction in BD’s pathophysiology [ 33 , 34 ], including its relationship with ROS [ 35 , 36 ]. An excess of ROS caused by an imbalance between oxidant production and antioxidant defenses is a key etiological factor in BD. In this review, we will explore how different mitochondrial dysfunctions—such as energy and ROS metabolism, mitochondrial quality control regarding mitophagy, and calcium signaling regarding synaptic plasticity—lead to ROS production. We will also examine how these mitochondrial dysfunctions have been reported in BD and review possible links among BD, mitochondrial dysfunctions, and ROS. Additionally, we will discuss potential novel therapeutic targets to improve these conditions. Take-Home Message • Mitochondria exhibit cell-type-specific traits, perform interconnected functions, and undergo dynamic, reversible physiological adjustments. • In neurons, mitochondria help maintain homeostasis by regulating energy production, ROS metabolism, calcium regulation, apoptosis, synaptic plasticity, and neurogenesis, which supports and protects neuronal function. • Multiple studies support the role of mitochondrial dysfunction in BD’s pathophysiology, especially its connection with ROS. We mainly focus on energy and reactive oxygen species metabolism, mitophagy, synaptic plasticity, and lipid metabolism, discussing their relationships with ROS and BD. Table 1 provides an overview of these topics.

[conclusion] 7. Conclusions We reviewed mitochondrial mechanisms and their link to dysfunction in BD and oxidative stress. We also discussed new treatments for BD targeting mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress reduction. The pathophysiology of BD is complex, and it remains unclear which mitochondrial mechanisms are altered first or whether multiple mechanisms are affected simultaneously during disease progression. A deeper understanding of mitochondrial function and dysfunction in BD could clarify how current drugs work and guide the development of therapies targeting specific mitochondrial processes. Various studies have been conducted [ 162 ]. More are underway to determine whether pharmacological treatments for modulating mitochondrial function can improve the prognosis of BD and explore the potential benefits of antioxidant substances. Therapies enhancing mitochondrial function are expected to serve as primary or adjunctive treatments, shedding light on new therapeutic options for BD, especially for those who do not fully respond to standard therapies.

retrieved_papers.title: Mood Monitoring, Mood Tracking, and Ambulatory Assessment Interventions in Depression and Bipolar Disorder: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2026]
[Abstract] Mood monitoring is widely used by people with depression and bipolar disorder (BD) to prevent relapse and improve insight into their condition, but it is unclear if these interventions have an impact on symptoms and for whom. As the capacity for passive mood monitoring increases, it is vital to improve our understanding of frequent mood assessment.
This systematic review and meta-analysis assessed the effect of mood monitoring interventions in people with depression and BD to decrease relapse risk and symptoms of depression and mania.
We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis (PROSPERO, International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews: CRD42023396473) and reported results according to PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analysis) guidelines. Randomized controlled trials with clinically important follow-up periods were identified via multiple database searches and rated for risk of bias using the Cochrane Risk of Bias tool. The primary outcomes were symptoms of depression and mania. Available data were pooled to calculate standardized mean differences (SMDs) for the primary outcomes: severity of depression, bipolar depression, and mania/hypomania.
We included 8 trials of 1230 participants and 6 different mood monitoring protocols. In BD, meta-analysis found a small but not statistically significant effect of mood monitoring interventions on decreasing mania symptoms (6 comparisons, n=873; SMD 0.16, 95% CI-0.34 to 0.01; P=.06) and no effect on bipolar depression (6 comparisons, n=873; SMD -0.08, 95% CI -0.31 to 0.15; P=.02). In depression, we found a small effect in decreasing symptoms of depression of borderline statistical significance at 12 months (2 comparisons, n=262; SMD -0.25, 95% CI -0.49 to 0.00; P=.05) but not at 6 months (2 comparisons, n=268; SMD -0.21, 95% CI -0.54 to 0.12; P=.21). There was an absence of evidence on the effect of mood monitoring on decreased relapse rates or readmission rates. Studies had a low risk of bias. There was no evidence on mood monitoring through ecological momentary assessment.
Overall mood monitoring interventions do not increase or decrease mood symptoms in people with BD, nor is there robust evidence of such effects in people with unipolar depression. Further research is merited on different forms of mood monitoring and to determine under what circumstances mood monitoring might have beneficial or adverse effects. These results initially suggest that ambulatory assessment does not induce large placebo effects or significantly negatively or positively affect mood, and thus that mood monitoring may be an appropriate outcome measure for research or for clinical practice.

retrieved_papers.title: Bipolar disorder in children, adolescents and young adults. Part 2. Therapeutic management. Recommendations under the patronage of the Executive Board of the Polish Psychiatric Association, National Consultants in the field of Psychiatry and National Consultants in Child and Adolescent Psychiatry.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] Bipolar disorder is characterized by recurrent episodes of depression, mania, hypomania, or mixed states. Early diagnosis and comprehensive treatment, including pharmacotherapy and psychosocial interactions, are associated with a more favorable prognosis. The general principles for the use of drugs in bipolar disorder in children and adolescents remain similar to those in adults. The basic drugs in bipolar disorder are mood stabilizers of the first and second generation; however, their efficacy and safety profile in children and adolescents differ from those in adults. In addition, prophylactic treatment is necessary to prevent recurrence. Lithium, aripiprazole, quetiapine, risperidone, olanzapine, asenapine, ziprasidone are recommended for the treatment of mania and mixed states. For the treatment of depressive episodes in bipolar disorder in children and adolescents, experts recommend lurasidone as monotherapy or olanzapine + fluoxetine as combination therapy. Although long-term treatment is a key aspect of bipolar disorder management in children and adolescents, consistent efficacy data are still lacking. Safety data indicate that the most commonly reported adverse reactions in children and adolescents treated with mood stabilizers are gastrointestinal and neurological adverse reactions, while the use of antipsychotics is mainly associated with weight gain and sedation.

retrieved_papers.title: Bipolar disorders.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2020]
[Abstract] Bipolar disorders are a complex group of severe and chronic disorders that includes bipolar I disorder, defined by the presence of a syndromal, manic episode, and bipolar II disorder, defined by the presence of a syndromal, hypomanic episode and a major depressive episode. Bipolar disorders substantially reduce psychosocial functioning and are associated with a loss of approximately 10-20 potential years of life. The mortality gap between populations with bipolar disorders and the general population is principally a result of excess deaths from cardiovascular disease and suicide. Bipolar disorder has a high heritability (approximately 70%). Bipolar disorders share genetic risk alleles with other mental and medical disorders. Bipolar I has a closer genetic association with schizophrenia relative to bipolar II, which has a closer genetic association with major depressive disorder. Although the pathogenesis of bipolar disorders is unknown, implicated processes include disturbances in neuronal-glial plasticity, monoaminergic signalling, inflammatory homoeostasis, cellular metabolic pathways, and mitochondrial function. The high prevalence of childhood maltreatment in people with bipolar disorders and the association between childhood maltreatment and a more complex presentation of bipolar disorder (eg, one including suicidality) highlight the role of adverse environmental exposures on the presentation of bipolar disorders. Although mania defines bipolar I disorder, depressive episodes and symptoms dominate the longitudinal course of, and disproportionately account for morbidity and mortality in, bipolar disorders. Lithium is the gold standard mood-stabilising agent for the treatment of people with bipolar disorders, and has antimanic, antidepressant, and anti-suicide effects. Although antipsychotics are effective in treating mania, few antipsychotics have proven to be effective in bipolar depression. Divalproex and carbamazepine are effective in the treatment of acute mania and lamotrigine is effective at treating and preventing bipolar depression. Antidepressants are widely prescribed for bipolar disorders despite a paucity of compelling evidence for their short-term or long-term efficacy. Moreover, antidepressant prescription in bipolar disorder is associated, in many cases, with mood destabilisation, especially during maintenance treatment. Unfortunately, effective pharmacological treatments for bipolar disorders are not universally available, particularly in low-income and middle-income countries. Targeting medical and psychiatric comorbidity, integrating adjunctive psychosocial treatments, and involving caregivers have been shown to improve health outcomes for people with bipolar disorders. The aim of this Seminar, which is intended mainly for primary care physicians, is to provide an overview of diagnostic, pathogenetic, and treatment considerations in bipolar disorders. Towards the foregoing aim, we review and synthesise evidence on the epidemiology, mechanisms, screening, and treatment of bipolar disorders.

[Introduction] Introduction N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptors are tetrameric ionotropic glutamate receptors found primarily in the brain and are densely located in areas, such as the cerebral cortex, hippocampus, nucleus accumbens, striatum, thalamus, amygdala, and brainstem [ 1 , 2 ]. Centrally, N-methyl-D-aspartate receptors (NMDARs) play integral roles in neurodevelopment and cognitive functions (e.g., learning and memory) through long-term potentiation as well as the induction of neural differentiation, neurogenesis, and synaptic plasticity [ 3 – 6 ]. At rest, magnesium ions extracellularly bind to the NMDAR preventing ion permeation through the receptor channel [ 7 , 8 ]. Upon glutamate binding, the magnesium block is removed allowing the influx of cations, principally calcium as well as sodium, contemporaneous with potassium efflux [ 6 – 11 ]. During long-term potentiation, the foregoing process results in the upregulation of α-amino-3-hydroxy-5-methyl-4-isoxazolepropionic acid receptors, another type of ionotropic glutamate receptor, which further amplifies glutamate-induced synaptic excitation [ 11 ]. Notwithstanding, individuals with depression are commonly reported to have disruptions in glutamatergic neurotransmission in which excessive NMDAR activation may be associated with neurodegeneration, excitotoxicity, and overall contributes to depression pathophysiology [ 12 ]. In addition to central NMDA function, NMDARs have been identified in other organs including the pancreas and stomach, wherein they modulate insulin and gastric acid secretion, respectively [ 13 – 16 ]. Extant literature indicates that pancreatic NMDARs are predominantly expressed in beta cells, which synthesize and release insulin, and are also located on alpha and delta cells that synthesize and release glucagon and somatostatin, respectively. Physiologically, NMDARs regulate pancreatic hormone release as well as beta cell viability and proliferation via modulation of intracellular calcium dynamics [ 13 , 16 ]. Preclinical studies suggest that overactivation NMDAR in beta cells may impair glucose-stimulated insulin secretion (GSIS) through excessive calcium influx and oxidative stress [ 17 ]. Therefore, aberrant glutamatergic signaling commonly observed in persons with depression may also have negative consequences on the function of peripheral organs, such as the pancreas. The presence of structural and functional connectivity between glutamate and insulin systems is further supported by evidence from epidemiological studies indicating that insulin resistance is commonly observed in persons with both bipolar disorder and major depressive disorder [ 18 , 19 ]. Moreover, persons with depressive disorders are at an increased risk of metabolic syndrome and type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) [ 20 , 21 ]. The foregoing trends support a conceptual framework that aberrant glutamate signaling may be a shared pathophysiological mechanism contributing to both mood dysregulation and impaired metabolic homeostasis in MDD. Not only have NMDAR antagonists (e.g., dextromethorphan, ketamine/esketamine) demonstrated rapid-acting antidepressant effects, preliminary in vivo studies investigating NMDAR antagonists (i.e., memantine and dextromethorphan) have observed modulation of blood-glucose levels via GSIS, as well as beta-cell proliferation and protection against stress-induced apoptosis [ 15 – 17 ]. Notwithstanding improvements in insulin secretion and control in diabetic animal models, the effects of NMDAR antagonism on pancreatic alpha and delta cells remains incompletely understood. Whether NMDAR antagonists, such as ketamine and esketamine, also exert differential effects on pancreatic islet cell viability and function has yet to be explored. Our systematic review aims to address this gap by synthesizing preclinical evidence on the effects of disparate NMDAR antagonists on pancreatic cell (i.e., alpha, beta, delta) viability and function (i.e., pancreatic hormone production and release).

[introduction] Introduction N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptors are tetrameric ionotropic glutamate receptors found primarily in the brain and are densely located in areas, such as the cerebral cortex, hippocampus, nucleus accumbens, striatum, thalamus, amygdala, and brainstem [ 1 , 2 ]. Centrally, N-methyl-D-aspartate receptors (NMDARs) play integral roles in neurodevelopment and cognitive functions (e.g., learning and memory) through long-term potentiation as well as the induction of neural differentiation, neurogenesis, and synaptic plasticity [ 3 – 6 ]. At rest, magnesium ions extracellularly bind to the NMDAR preventing ion permeation through the receptor channel [ 7 , 8 ]. Upon glutamate binding, the magnesium block is removed allowing the influx of cations, principally calcium as well as sodium, contemporaneous with potassium efflux [ 6 – 11 ]. During long-term potentiation, the foregoing process results in the upregulation of α-amino-3-hydroxy-5-methyl-4-isoxazolepropionic acid receptors, another type of ionotropic glutamate receptor, which further amplifies glutamate-induced synaptic excitation [ 11 ]. Notwithstanding, individuals with depression are commonly reported to have disruptions in glutamatergic neurotransmission in which excessive NMDAR activation may be associated with neurodegeneration, excitotoxicity, and overall contributes to depression pathophysiology [ 12 ]. In addition to central NMDA function, NMDARs have been identified in other organs including the pancreas and stomach, wherein they modulate insulin and gastric acid secretion, respectively [ 13 – 16 ]. Extant literature indicates that pancreatic NMDARs are predominantly expressed in beta cells, which synthesize and release insulin, and are also located on alpha and delta cells that synthesize and release glucagon and somatostatin, respectively. Physiologically, NMDARs regulate pancreatic hormone release as well as beta cell viability and proliferation via modulation of intracellular calcium dynamics [ 13 , 16 ]. Preclinical studies suggest that overactivation NMDAR in beta cells may impair glucose-stimulated insulin secretion (GSIS) through excessive calcium influx and oxidative stress [ 17 ]. Therefore, aberrant glutamatergic signaling commonly observed in persons with depression may also have negative consequences on the function of peripheral organs, such as the pancreas. The presence of structural and functional connectivity between glutamate and insulin systems is further supported by evidence from epidemiological studies indicating that insulin resistance is commonly observed in persons with both bipolar disorder and major depressive disorder [ 18 , 19 ]. Moreover, persons with depressive disorders are at an increased risk of metabolic syndrome and type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) [ 20 , 21 ]. The foregoing trends support a conceptual framework that aberrant glutamate signaling may be a shared pathophysiological mechanism contributing to both mood dysregulation and impaired metabolic homeostasis in MDD. Not only have NMDAR antagonists (e.g., dextromethorphan, ketamine/esketamine) demonstrated rapid-acting antidepressant effects, preliminary in vivo studies investigating NMDAR antagonists (i.e., memantine and dextromethorphan) have observed modulation of blood-glucose levels via GSIS, as well as beta-cell proliferation and protection against stress-induced apoptosis [ 15 – 17 ]. Notwithstanding improvements in insulin secretion and control in diabetic animal models, the effects of NMDAR antagonism on pancreatic alpha and delta cells remains incompletely understood. Whether NMDAR antagonists, such as ketamine and esketamine, also exert differential effects on pancreatic islet cell viability and function has yet to be explored. Our systematic review aims to address this gap by synthesizing preclinical evidence on the effects of disparate NMDAR antagonists on pancreatic cell (i.e., alpha, beta, delta) viability and function (i.e., pancreatic hormone production and release).

[methods] Methods Search String and Databases To investigate the effects of NMDA receptor antagonists on pancreatic cells, we conducted a systematic review in accordance with the 2020 Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) (see online suppl. material; for all online suppl. material, see https://doi.org/10.1159/000549276 for 2020 PRISMA Checklist) [ 22 ]. The protocol for this review has not been registered. A systematic search was conducted on PubMed and Ovid databases for all relevant literature published from database inception to September 11, 2024. Additional manual searches were conducted on Google Scholar, Scopus, and citation searching. The following search string was used for all included databases: ([NMDA Antagonist*] OR [NMDA Receptor Antagonist*] OR [N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor antagonist*] OR [Ketamine] OR [Esketamine] OR [Dextromethorphan] OR [Dextrorphan] OR [Memantine] OR [Phencyclidine] OR [Amantadine]) AND ([Pancreatic Islets] OR [Pancreatic cells] OR [Alpha cells] OR [Beta cells] OR [Delta cells] OR [Islets of Langerhans]). No language restrictions or filters were applied during the search process. Eligibility Criteria The identified studies were considered to be eligible for inclusion if they met all of the following inclusion criteria: (1) a primary preclinical or clinical study, (2) investigated a NMDAR antagonist as an intervention, (3) must be a controlled study, (4) reports at least one functional or viability-related outcome in pancreatic cells, including but not limited to pancreatic islet viability and/or apoptosis, cell proliferation, pancreatic hormone production and secretion, islet activation, and/or islet morphology, (5) must be published in English or be translated to English by the publisher. Adequate control(s) or comparators were considered as vehicle controls/sham-exposed controls or active controls (i.e., we did not include studies if they only performed within-subject comparisons). Studies were excluded if they met any of the following exclusion criteria: (1) not a primary study (e.g., reviews, commentaries, letter to the editor, dissertations, conference abstracts, etc), (2) case studies or case series, (3) does include a NMDAR antagonist as an investigational agent, (4) no comparator or control group, (5) does not report on any of the described functional or viability-related pancreatic outcomes, (6) not published in or translated to English, (7) no full-text availability including conference abstracts. If the full-text was not available, corresponding authors were contacted. A summary of the eligibility criteria is detailed in Table 1 . Table 1. Eligibility criteria ​ Inclusion criteria Exclusion criteria Population Human studies In vitro study or an in vivo study N/A Intervention/exposure Any NMDA receptor antagonist (e.g., ketamine, dextromethorphan, dextrorphan, MK-801, etc.) Does not include investigation of a NMDA receptor antagonist Comparator/context Must be a controlled study (i.e., vehicle control and/or active control) No comparator or control Outcome Investigation of the effects of a NMDA receptor antagonist on pancreatic cells (e.g., cell viability, insulin secretion, glucose-stimulated insulin secretion, pancreatic cell morphology, etc.) Does not evaluate the effects of NMDA receptor antagonist(s) on pancreatic cells or pancreatic function Study characteristics In vitro or in vivo study Randomized controlled trials Observational studies Cohort studies Non-primary research articles (i.e., systematic reviews, narrative reviews, meta-analyses, letters to the editor, commentaries, conference abstracts) Case studies or case series Other Must be published in English Not published in English No full-text availability Study Screening All retrieved studies were screened independently by two reviewers (E.Y. and J.U.) using the Covidence platform [ 23 ]. Studies first underwent title and abstract screening and studies deemed relevant by at least one reviewer subsequently underwent full-text screening. Studies that underwent full-text screening were screened against the eligibility criteria outlined in Table 1 . Briefly, studies were eligible for inclusion if they were a primary, controlled study evaluating the effects of a NMDA receptor antagonist on the function and viability of pancreatic islets either in vitro or in vivo. Studies were excluded if they were not a primary study, were not controlled, did not evaluate a NMDA receptor antagonist, or did not report on pancreatic islet function or viability as an outcome measure. Studies must have received a unanimous decision for inclusion by both reviewers. Any discrepancies were resolved through discussion and underwent review by a third reviewer (S.W.). Data Extraction and Synthesis Data were extracted from included studies using a piloted data extraction table by two reviewers (S.W. and G.H.L.). Data that was to be extracted was established a priori and included: (1) author and publication date, (2) sample size and type (i.e., cells, species, humans), (3) NMDA antagonist of interest, (4) dose, frequency and route of administration of NMDA antagonist, (5) measurement tools to assess effects on pancreatic cells, (6) primary and secondary results of interest. Any discrepancies were resolved through discussion. Missing and unclear outcome data were handled by extracting results as presented and would be noted as incomplete in the synthesis. Due to the heterogeneity of the results, a quantitative analysis could not be conducted and results were synthesized and presented in a descriptive manner. Specifically, we grouped studies thematically by islet type (whole islets, alpha, beta delta) and presented by functional domains in the following order: proliferation, viability and stress responses, depolarization and activation, and hormone production and secretion. To evaluate the direction and consistency of the results across the included studies, we conducted vote-counting based on the direction of the effect, supplemented by a qualitative discussion of methodological differences. Risk of Bias Assessment To assess the included studies for a potential risk of bias, a modified version of the SYRCLE risk of bias tool was utilized [ 24 , 25 ]. The modified SYRCLE risk of bias tool was developed by De la Rosa González et al. [ 25 ] to be adapted for in vitro research to account for differences in cell lines and intervention variability. In addition, the tool was adjusted to focus on transparency and objectivity of outcome assessment and declaration of interests and the availability of data for independent verification. All studies were assessed independently by two reviewers (S.W. and G.H.L.). Any discrepancies were resolved through discussion.

[results] Risk of Bias Results From the risk of bias assessment, the most common sources of potential bias were inadequate description on sample size justifications as well as whether investigators and study assessors were blinded to treatment allocation. Specifically, these limitations introduce the potential for selection and detection bias. As thirteen out of the fourteen studies did not include their power analysis, it is difficult to ascertain whether the included samples are sufficiently large to detect a clinically significant effect. Therefore, results reported herein should be considered as exploratory. While blinding is not regularly implemented for in vitro and in vivo studies, this may affect outcome assessment and data validity. Specific ratings for the risk of bias assessment of each component study are outlined in Table 2 .

[discussion] Discussion Herein, our systematic review indicates that NMDA receptor antagonists may dose- and time-dependently affect pancreatic cell viability, proliferation, activation, and function. Converging lines of evidence suggest that dextromethorphan, dextrorphan, MK-801, memantine and ketamine negatively modulate concentrations of apoptotic, inflammatory, and ER stress response factors, with corresponding improvements in islet survival and hormone secretion. Protection of both alpha and beta cells was concurrent with improvements in insulin secretion without affecting basal insulin levels, suggesting NMDA receptor antagonism primarily enhances stimulus-dependent insulin release. Moreover, preliminary evidence indicates that NMDA receptor-mediated improvements in glucose control were independent of glucagon. The foregoing observations may subserve the improved insulin and glucose control demonstrated in the animal studies and the singular study investigating oral dextromethorphan in persons with type 2 diabetes. However, the effects of NMDA receptor-mediated improvements in cell viability and function were not uniform. Specifically, higher doses (e.g., 100 μm dextromethorphan, anesthetic doses of ketamine) or prolonged exposure (e.g., 48 h) decreased cell viability and decreased insulin gene expression. This variability highlights the need for additional rigorous in vivo studies with standardized dosing, outcome measures and models to disaggregate potential drug-specific effects of NMDA receptor antagonism on islet viability and function. Dysregulated insulin signaling has been increasingly implicated in the pathophysiology of mood disorders (e.g., major depressive disorder, bipolar disorder). For example, core symptoms of depressive disorders that are inadequately treated by conventional antidepressants, such as anhedonia, reward, and cognitive function, are known to be affected by glucose tolerance and insulin signaling [ 39 – 42 ]. Although improved insulin signaling may influence symptoms domains, there is a paucity of direct clinical evidence. While NMDA receptor antagonists are emerging agents for depression treatment, the extent to which NMDA receptor modulation in islets contributes to central antidepressant efficacy also remains unclear. Future studies should aim to test whether peripheral islet modulation and insulin signaling represent an additional pathway by which NMDA receptor antagonists exert their antidepressant effects. There are methodological limitations that may affect the inferences and interpretations in our systematic review. Primarily, there is a paucity of human studies reporting on the effects of varying doses of NMDA receptor antagonists on pancreatic islets. The majority of the included studies were conducted in vitro and in animal models, often with small sample sizes and variable methodological quality. As culture conditions cannot fully recapitulate in vivo physiology, there is limited external validity. Moreover, heterogeneity in models (e.g., primary islets from varying rodent strains, pancreatic cancer cell lines), dosing regimens (e.g., drug, dose, incubation times), and outcome measures limits the generalizability and comparability of results. Therefore, the results reported herein may not directly translate to disparate human populations. Taken together, these methodological constraints underscore that current evidence should be regarded as hypothesis-generating rather than conclusive. Second, as our systematic review focused on the effects of NMDA receptor antagonism on pancreatic cells, most of the studies involved in vitro procedures in which there is currently no validated risk of bias tool. As such, we utilized a modified risk of bias tool, which may not fully capture potential sources of bias. While the tool assesses methods for randomization, blinding and allocation concealment, these are not standard reporting items within preclinical studies. In addition, the tool does not assess for the presence of publication bias, inter-rater reliability or model validity, thus limiting the internal validity of the studies. In addition, there is substantial pharmacodynamic heterogeneity between glutamate signaling modulators, consequently, it cannot be assumed that other agents with direct and/or indirect effects on NMDA signaling (e.g., D-cycloserine) will be comparable to other glutamate modulators [ 43 ]. Finally, due to between-study heterogeneity in the outcome measures, we were not able to conduct a quantitative analysis of our intended aims. Finally, despite the expression of NMDA receptors on pancreatic delta cells, which secrete somatostatin and play a key regulatory role in paracrine signaling, our review did not identify any studies evaluating the effects of NMDA receptor antagonism on delta cell viability or function. This represents a significant gap in literature as somatostatin also contributes to glucose-insulin homeostasis. The absence of delta cell outcomes limits our ability to draw comprehensive conclusions regarding islet responses to NMDA receptor antagonism. Future preclinical studies should explicitly evaluate delta cell outcomes to determine whether their functions are directly and/or indirectly affected.

[conclusion] Conclusion Overall, converging lines of evidence suggests that NMDA receptor antagonism may dose- and time-dependently improve pancreatic cell viability, proliferation, and insulin response both in vitro and in animal models. The effects of NMDA receptor antagonism on pancreatic islets are not yet fit-for-purpose, but underscores the need for additional rigorous in vivo studies with standardized dosing, models, and outcomes measures. Investigations of NMDA receptor antagonists on delta cell viability and function are required. Moreover, the evaluation of the effects of peripheral NMDA receptor antagonism on central insulin control is a future research vista.

retrieved_papers.title: Verapamil treatment for women with bipolar disorder.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2002]
[Abstract] Additional pharmacological treatments are needed for patients with bipolar disorder. We describe our experience with verapamil in an inclusive, sequential series of outpatient women (some pregnant) with bipolar disorder.
All women who were prescribed verapamil for bipolar disorder (n = 37) were included. We used the criterion of 50% reduction in scores on the Mania Rating Scale or the Hamilton Rating Scale for Depression to define response for women who were treated for an acute episode of bipolar hypomania/mania or depression, respectively. For euthymic women who chose verapamil maintenance treatment, we evaluated whether they met criteria for a recurrent episode during therapy.
Treatment for acute episodes was initiated in 28 women. Of women with depression and mania, 39% and 100% responded, respectively. Seven of the nine patients (77%) with mixed states responded: all seven improved to response criterion on the mania scale, and two responded on the depression scales as well. Six of eight patients who received continuation therapy remained well.
These data provide evidence that verapamil is effective for mania. The response rate for mania compares favorably to that for other mood stabilizers. After decades of case reports and underpowered clinical trials, we must definitively study verapamil for efficacy and gender specificity in bipolar disorder.

retrieved_papers.title: [Calcium antagonists and lithium in preventive treatment of manic-depressive disorder].
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 1996]
[Abstract] Recent advances in knowledge about calcium's role as an intracellular regulator allow to broaden understanding of possible pathophysiologic mechanisms in affective disorders. An hypothesis emerge that bipolar illness arise from disorders in calcium-regulated functions. Given this hypothesis, some authors propose to describe the common profiles of action of the different mood-stabilizers: all the neural mechanisms that are hypothesized to explain various psychopharmacological treatments of bipolar illness involve functions that are critically controlled by calcium. Moreover, in every instance, a known action of lithium on calcium-dependent processes could account for lithium's prophylactic results. Similarities exist between the action of lithium and calcium antagonists like verapamil and nimodipine. From these considerations the hypothesis emerge that calcium antagonists could be an alternative pharmacological agent in the maintenance treatment of bipolar illness. Calcium antagonists have been found useful in this indication by a number of investigators. Given the safety and relative lack of side effects of calcium channel blocking agents, their potential efficacy in mood disorders, it is concluded that calcium antagonists may be an alternative choice in prophylactic treatment for bipolar illness, especially in patients who cannot be treated with lithium or carbamazepine. There is evidence for using verapamil at 240 to 320 mg a day, in 2 to 4 times. Some studies suggest that the association of lithium with calcium antagonist resulted more effective than lithium alone or calcium antagonist alone in the reduction of episodes of affective disorders. However, concomitant administration of a calcium channel antagonist and lithium present adverse interactions (lithium toxicity, cardiovascular accidents), probably because of synergistic toxic effects. Therefore, authors advise care in monitoring patients receiving the combination of these medications.

retrieved_papers.title: Peripheral cytokine levels across psychiatric disorders: A systematic review and network meta-analysis.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2023]
[Abstract] Immune dysregulated cytokine production is involved in mental diseases. However, the results are inconsistent and the pattern of cytokine alterations has not been compared across disorders. We performed a network impact analysis of cytokine levels for different psychiatric disorders including schizophrenia, major depressive disorder, bipolar disorder, panic disorder, post-traumatic stress disorder and obsessive compressive disorder to evaluate their clinical impact across conditions. Studies were identified by searching the electronic databases up to 31/05/2022. A total of eight cytokines, together with (high-sensitivity) C-reactive proteins (hsCRP/CRP) were included in the network meta-analysis. The levels of proinflammatory cytokines, hsCRP/CRP and interleukin 6 (IL-6) were significantly increased in patients with psychiatric disorders when compared to controls. IL-6 showed no significant difference among comparisons between disorders according to the network meta-analysis. Interleukin 10 (IL-10) is significantly increased in patients with bipolar disorder compared to major depressive disorder. Further, the level of interleukin-1 beta (IL-1β) was significantly increased in major depressive disorder as compared to bipolar disorder. The level of interleukin 8 (IL-8) varied among these psychiatric disorders based on the network meta-analysis result. Overall, abnormal cytokine levels were found in psychiatric disorders, and some of the cytokines displayed differential characteristics in these disorders, especially IL-8, pointing to a role as potential biomarkers for general and differential diagnosis.

[Introduction] Background This study was a randomized controlled trial investigating the effects of a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) and improvement in depressive symptoms on innate immunity and inflammation in people with HIV (PWH).

[introduction] Background This study was a randomized controlled trial investigating the effects of a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) and improvement in depressive symptoms on innate immunity and inflammation in people with HIV (PWH).

[methods] Methods and Materials Participants Participants were recruited from multiple settings in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, including HIV and mental health clinics, community-based organizations, and outreach to groups including PWH. Participants were included if they met the following criteria: 1) ages 18 to 70; 2) seropositive for HIV (perinatal acquisition excluded); 3) on a regimen of antiretroviral therapy (ART) for at least 3 months; 4) HIV RNA <200 copies/mL; 5) 17-item Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HAMD-17) score ≥13; 6) diagnosis of major depressive disorder (MDD), persistent depressive disorder, unspecified depressive disorder, or other specified depressive disorder; 7) willing and able to participate with antidepressant medication regimen and scheduled follow-up visits; and 8) able to understand and provide informed consent. Participants were excluded if they met any of the following criteria: 1) acute suicidal ideation, gestures, or attempts; 2) significant cognitive impairment; 3) current bipolar disorder (I or II), schizophrenia, presence of psychotic symptoms, or known diagnosis of a primary psychotic disorder; 4) currently taking antipsychotic medication; 5) current pharmacotherapy for depression; 6) immunization with trial HIV vaccine; 7) currently pregnant, within 9 months postdelivery, and/or breastfeeding; 8) history of seizure disorder or taking carbamazepine; 9) renal failure; 10) history of acute narrow/closed-angle glaucoma; 11) a current or chronic medical condition that would likely preclude adherence to protocol or completion of trial; 12) use of immune-altering medication within 4 weeks prior to enrollment (except acyclovir and related antiviral medications, corticosteroids, or statin medications); 13) history of intolerance or nonresponse to an adequate trial of SSRI; 14) taken monoamine oxidase inhibitors within 14 days; 15) taking linezolid or intravenous methylene blue; 16) on a regular regimen of anticoagulant medication; 17) currently taking central nervous system drugs (except gabapentin, pregabalin, varenicline, antihistamines, and hypnotics); 18) on any triptan medications; or 19) undergoing electroconvulsive therapy. Individuals not eligible were referred for care outside of the research setting as clinically indicated. The protocol was approved by the University of Pennsylvania Institutional Review Board. All participants provided written informed consent after thorough review of the informed consent form and discussion of study procedures with research staff. Participants were reimbursed for their time and travel (maximum $380 across 10 weeks). Procedures Study activities occurred in Philadelphia from February 2017 to January 2022 (see Figure 1 ). Prior to enrollment, study staff screened each participant, who received a comprehensive medical and psychiatric assessment, including plasma HIV RNA measurement, urine pregnancy test in premenopausal women with no history of tubal ligation, the International HIV Dementia Scale, and the Structured Clinical Interview for DSM-5 diagnoses ( 35 ). Depression symptom severity was evaluated with the HAMD-17. Figure 1 CONSORT (Consolidated Standards of Reporting Trials) flow diagram for study screening, enrollment, randomization, and completion. PI, principal investigator. To minimize potential circadian and activity/exertional effects on immune measures, blood samples were collected in almost all cases in the morning after participants had fasted since midnight and rested for at least 30 minutes. The research pharmacy randomized participants according to a list generated by the study statistician (using PROC PLAN in SAS/STAT software; version 8) to 10 weeks of double-blinded therapy with either the SSRI escitalopram or placebo. Stratification was based on gender and baseline depression symptom severity (i.e., those with HAMD-17 scores ≤19 or ≥20) and randomizations within each stratum were balanced in blocks of size 8. No study staff with participant contact knew of the treatment assignment prior to unblinding at each participant’s final study visit. Only the research pharmacy remained unblinded to treatment condition. Escitalopram was selected for its efficacy and pharmacological properties. A meta-analysis concluded that escitalopram was one of only 2 (along with sertraline) newer-generation antidepressants that were superior in terms of both efficacy and tolerability ( 36 ). Furthermore, it was considered well suited to a study of medically complex patients because of relatively simple dosing and few drug-drug interactions. Eligible participants were enrolled in the RCT by study psychiatrists, who prescribed 10 mg of escitalopram or placebo equivalent daily and evaluated participants weekly from baseline to week 6 and at weeks 8 and 10. Upward titration was permitted after 1 week based on tolerability and clinical response, and downward titration was permitted if side effects were severe. Because of ethical concerns regarding the use of placebo in a potentially vulnerable group, all participants concurrently received computer-assisted cognitive behavior therapy (CCBT) in combination with pharmacotherapy. CCBT was conducted using the Good Days Ahead program ( 37 ). This model paired baseline and biweekly sessions with a cognitive therapist and weekly web-based modules that teach CBT skills through interactive exercises. This form of CCBT has been shown to be noninferior to, and significantly more cost-effective than, conventional CBT in one RCT ( 38 , 39 ) and was found to be significantly more effective than treatment as usual in one study of depression in primary care ( 40 , 41 , 42 ). Because of changes in health care delivery during the COVID-19 pandemic, participant visits after March 16, 2020, were conducted remotely as needed. Immune Assessment Seventy milliliters of peripheral blood were drawn from each participant at baseline and weeks 2, 4, and 10. For plasma and sera collection, tubes were centrifuged within 2 hours of collection, and aliquots of plasma and sera were frozen and stored at −80 °C. Peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) for icIFN-γ assay were isolated from the heparin tube by the standard Ficoll-Paque separation technique (GE Healthcare) within 2 hours of collection, frozen in fetal bovine serum (FBS) 10% DMSO, and stored in liquid nitrogen. CD4 and CD8 counts were performed on the day of collection using BD Multitest CD3/CD8/CD45/CD4 reagent (#340499; BD Biosciences), SPHERO AccuCount Particles (#ACURFP-50-10; Spherotech Inc.), and BD FACS Lysing Solution (#349202; BD Biosciences). Complete blood count (CBC), comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP), and HIV viral load were performed by Quest Diagnostics (CBC [includes differential and platelets; #6399]; CMP [#102310]; HIV-1 RNA, Quantitative, Real-Time PCR [#40085], lowest level of quantification 20 copies/mL). Plasma levels of IL-6 and CRP and analytic performance verification were conducted on the ProteinSimple Ella immunoassay platform (Bio-Techne). NK Cell Activity Assay NK cell activity was assessed using standard techniques that have been well established and utilized in our investigations ( 31 , 34 ). All samples were processed within 24 hours of sampling. NK cell functions were assessed at baseline and after 2, 4, and 10 weeks in the study by the standard chromium release assay, using K562 target cells and PBMCs at effector to target cell ratios of 6.25 to 50. Percent lysis values were normalized for percent NK cells within the PBMC preparations expressed as LUNKs as previously described ( 34 ). icIFN-γ Assay icIFN-γ was assessed as described ( 43 ), with some modifications in stimulated PBMCs at baseline and after 2, 4, and 10 weeks. Cryopreserved PBMCs were defrosted, at all 4 time points from the same patient simultaneously, and 500,000 cells were incubated for 18 hours in Roswell Park Memorial Institute medium + 10% FBS with IL-2, 100 U/mL (#354043; BD Biosciences), and IL-12, 5 ng/mL (#Z02709; GenScript), at 37 °C 5% CO 2 . Cells were treated with 10 μg/mL brefeldin (#57651; Sigma) and GolgiStop (#554724; BD Biosciences) during the last 3 hours of culture; fixed with BD Fixation/Permeabilization Solution (#554722; BD Biosciences), washed with BD Perm/Wash Buffer (#554723; BD Biosciences), and stained with the following antibodies: APC/Cy7 antihuman CD45 antibody (Clone: HI30) (#304014; BioLegend), PerCP antihuman CD3 antibody (Clone: UCHT1) (#300428; BioLegend), PE/Cy7 antihuman CD56 antibody (Clone: HCD56) (#318318; BioLegend), PE antihuman IFN-γ antibody (#554701; BD Biosciences), LIVE/DEAD Fixable Blue Dead Cell Stain Kit (# L23105 ; Life Technologies). The number of IFN-γ + NK cells was assessed by flow cytometry gated on CD45 + /CD3 − /CD56 + cells. Statistical Analysis The primary outcomes of LUNKs, icIFN-γ in NK cells, IL-6, and CRP were obtained at baseline and at weeks 2, 4, and 10, and the HAMD-17 was obtained at baseline and weeks 1 to 6, 8, and 10. The primary outcomes were log 10 transformed prior to analyses to reduce levels of skewness. For the primary analyses, the groups were compared using linear mixed-effects models, with random intercepts to account for within-participant correlations over time. For each of the 4 immunity and inflammation responses, examination of discrete time models showed that a continuous linear trend was sufficient to capture patterns of change. Thus, the models included a binary variable for randomization group (escitalopram vs. placebo), a continuous variable for week, and a group by week interaction term, with a centered version of the (log 10 transformed) baseline response as a baseline covariate. To account for the stratified randomization, the models also included binary variables for gender and depression severity. To control type 1 error rate within the 2 immunity and 2 inflammation hypotheses, an alpha level of 0.025 was used as a threshold for significance. Similar linear mixed-effects models were used to compare the groups on change on HAMD-17 scores across the 10 weeks of treatment, as a linear trend was again sufficient to capture patterns of change. Spearman rank correlations were used to examine whether baseline to week-10 change on the immunity and inflammation measures were related to change in depression scores. These analyses were repeated using the self-report Beck Depression Inventory (BDI). The methods of Hedeker et al. ( 44 ) were used to determine sample size, based on 2-sided hypothesis tests, with an assumed 10% loss to attrition over the 10 weeks. A within-person correlation of 0.6 between visits was assumed, and the intended sample of 180 provided 80% power to detect a main effect (from week 2 through week 10) of d = 0.41 or higher or a linear group × time effect (from week 0 through week 10) of Cohen’s d = 0.42 or higher and 80% power. The attained sample had 55 in the escitalopram group and 53 in the placebo group, with 22% and 12% dropout, respectively, and an observed within-participant correlation of 0.37; this yielded 80% power for a group effect of d = 0.52 and a group × time effect of d = 0.66.

[results] Results The sample included 108 participants (see Table 1 ). The mean (SD) age was 49.9 (11.5) years; 61% of the sample self-identified as cisgender male, and 88% self-identified as Black. For education level, 18% did not have a high school diploma/equivalent, 29% completed high school, 38% had some college, 14% completed college or beyond, and 2% were unknown. There were no significant differences between escitalopram and placebo groups in age, gender, race, or baseline depression severity. The mean HAMD-17 score at baseline was 19.1 (4.9) overall, 19.1 (4.90) in the escitalopram group, and 19.2 (5.1) in the placebo group. Table 1 Baseline Sample Characteristics Full Sample, N = 108 Escitalopram, n = 55 Placebo, n = 53 Demographic Measures Age, Years 49.91 (11.45) 49.95 (11.99) 49.87 (10.97) Gender Woman 40 (37%) 20 (37%) 20 (38%) Man 66 (61%) 34 (61%) 32 (60%) Transgender woman 2 (2%) 1 (2%) 1 (1%) Race Black 95 (88%) 49 (89%) 46 (87%) Multiracial, Asian and White 1 (1%) 0 (0%) 1 (2%) Multiracial, Black and American Indian 2 (2%) 1 (2%) 1 (2%) Unknown 1 (1%) 0 (0%) 1 (2%) White 9 (8%) 5 (9%) 4 (8%) Ethnicity Hispanic/Latinx 11 (10%) 4 (7%) 7 (13%) Not Hispanic/Latinx 96 (89%) 50 (91%) 46 (87%) Unknown 1 (1%) 1 (2%) 0 (0%) Education Level Less than high school 19 (18%) 8 (15%) 11 (21%) High school 31 (29%) 16 (29%) 15 (28%) Some college 41 (38%) 23 (42%) 18 (34%) College+ 15 (14%) 7 (13%) 8 (15%) Unknown 2 (2%) 1 (2%) 1 (2%) Psychopathology HAMD-17 19.17 (4.96) 19.13 (4.90) 19.21 (5.07) Immunology Measures IL-6, pcg/ml 2.92 (1.65–4.96) [13] 2.75 (1.64–3.77) [9] 2.95 (1.68–4.63) [4] LUNK 246.35 (136.69–413.57) [0] 211.74 (127.30–348.70) [0] 275.57 (142.11–461.35) [0] CRP, mg/L 3.03 (0.98–7.15) [14] 1.98 (0.86–5.87) [9] 3.82 (1.06–8.50) [5] IFN-γ+, % of total NK cells 6.55% (3.20%–12.15%) [12] 7.70% (3.60%–13.10%) [9] 6.40% (3.10%–12.00%) [3] Values are presented as mean (SD), n (%), or median (IQR) [number of missing values]. Cell entries are for the total sample; escitalopram and placebo columns show mean (SD) for continuous measures and counts (%) for categorical measures. CRP, C-reactive protein; HAMD-17, 17-item Hamilton Depression Rating Scale; IFN-γ, interferon gamma; IL-6, interleukin 6; LUNK, lytic units per 10 7 NK cells; NK, natural killer. By week 10, 65.4% of participants were taking 10 mg/day and 34.5% were taking 20 mg/day of escitalopram or placebo equivalent. Most participants did not receive an increase in dose before week 4, and some remained on the starting dose throughout the protocol. Missing Data For the primary immunologic outcomes, dropout was almost entirely concentrated between baseline and week 2, with almost full retention between week 2 and week 10. For the placebo group ( n = 53 at baseline), we obtained responses for 47 participants (88.7%) at week 2 and 47 (88.7%) and 46 participants (86.8%) at weeks 4 and 10, respectively; for the escitalopram group ( n = 55 at baseline), we obtained responses for 43 participants (78.2%) at week 2 and 45 participants (81.8%) and 43 participants (78.2%) at weeks 4 and 10, respectively. The odds of week 2 dropout in the escitalopram group was 2.19 times the odds in the placebo group (95% CI, 0.75–6.33; p = .22). Primary Outcomes There were no statistically significant effects on any of the tests of the primary hypotheses (see Table 2 ). Differences in only one of the immune measures—CRP—approached statistical significance in the predicted direction ( F 1,172 = 3.41, p = .067). Moreover, none of the immune parameters showed significant effects for time, indicating that, despite a large reduction in the participants’ level of depression (see below), there was no evidence of improvement in immune status (see Table 2 ). Table 2 Repeated Immunologic Measures by Group and Study Week Week Group Log 10 (IL-6) Log 10 (LUNK) Log 10 (CRP) Log 10 (IFN-γ-NK) 0 Placebo 0.51 (0.37) 2.38 (0.37) 0.48 (0.62) 0.78 (0.38) 0 Escitalopram 0.41 (0.29) 2.31 (0.35) 0.30 (0.55) 0.86 (0.39) 2 Placebo 0.53 (0.28) 2.34 (0.28) 0.56 (0.57) 0.72 (0.40) 2 Escitalopram 0.43 (0.31) 2.35 (0.42) 0.23 (0.48) 0.88 (0.43) 4 Placebo 0.50 (0.28) 2.37 (0.27) 0.51 (0.58) 0.82 (0.36) 4 Escitalopram 0.45 (0.33) 2.31 (0.36) 0.40 (0.54) 0.84 (0.40) 10 Placebo 0.47 (0.31) 2.37 (0.32) 0.41 (0.52) 0.79 (0.35) 10 Escitalopram 0.49 (0.36) 2.37 (0.34) 0.39 (0.52) 0.87 (0.37) Values are presented as response mean (SD) for the log 10 -transformed immunologic responses for each study week. CRP, C-reactive protein; IL-6, interleukin 6; IFN-γ-NK, interferon gamma in NK cells; LUNK, lytic units per 10 7 NK cells; NK, natural killer. Secondary Outcomes Participants showed a significant reduction in depressive symptoms from pretreatment to posttreatment, with a final mean HAMD-17 score of 8.0 (7.6) in the pooled sample ( F 1,667 = 220.70, p < .001). In the placebo group, the average HAMD-17 score decreased from 18.9 (4.9) at baseline to 8.8 (8.0) at week 10, with an average decrease of 10.1 (7.7); in the escitalopram group, the average HAMD-17 score went from 19.2 (4.7) to 7.1 (7.3) with an average decrease of 12.1 (7.7). The entire sample had an average decrease of 11.0 (7.7). However, there was no differential effect for treatment, whether viewed as a group × time interaction ( F 1,166 = 0.00, p = .976) or a main effect of group ( F 1,667 = 0.50, p = .479). Most participants (67.3% [4.7%]) experienced a 50% reduction in HAMD-17 scores, a commonly used way to gauge treatment response, with rates of 64.7% (3.4%) in the placebo group and 70.8% (3.1%) in the escitalopram group, which were not significantly different from each other (relative risk = 1.1; 95% CI, 0.8–1.4; p = .51). There was no evidence that the magnitude of symptom improvement was associated with changes in immune measures. Spearman correlations between decreases from pre- to posttreatment on the 4 immunity and inflammation measures and the corresponding decrease on the HAMD-17 score were small, and none were statistically significant: LUNKs ( r = −0.07, p = .533), IFN-γ ( r = 0.06, p = .590), IL-6 ( r = −0.00, p = .974), and CRP ( r = −0.18, p = .090). The same overall pattern of results was observed on analyses using the BDI as the measure of depression; despite a significant reduction in depressive symptoms, there were no significant associations between changes in depressive symptoms and immune parameters (see the Supplement ).

[discussion] Discussion This study was designed to further elucidate the associations between depressive symptoms and immunologic function in PWH during a course of antidepressant treatment. These associations have important health implications because many PWH suffer from depression, and there is growing evidence that depression adversely affects several aspects of immune function. In the current study, we were particularly interested in the impact of treatment with an SSRI on immune function. More specifically, we were investigating whether pharmacotherapy with escitalopram—one of the most commonly prescribed SSRIs—had a specific immune modulating effect or whether treatment effects on immune function were largely nonspecific (i.e., a consequence of symptomatic improvement). To our knowledge, this study is the first double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of an SSRI for treating depression in HIV with a focus on innate immunity and inflammation. The study participants were generalizable in that they were receiving ongoing care for HIV and had a broad range of depressive symptom severity and prior treatment experience. To ensure that participants were adherent to antiviral treatment, we limited enrollment to people showing suppression of viral expression. Our results were not supportive of the several hypotheses that guided the design of the study, even though the average participant experienced a substantial reduction in depressive symptoms across 10 weeks of treatment. Interpretation of these findings warrants consideration of several potentially moderating or confounding factors, starting with the fact that the participants who were treated with escitalopram did not experience statistically significantly greater symptom relief than the participants who received placebo. Several reasons may explain why escitalopram did not show the expected degree of antidepressant efficacy in this trial. First, although escitalopram has one of the better track records for demonstrating efficacy in placebo-controlled clinical trials ( 45 ), a certain proportion of studies of all antidepressants with established efficacy fail to detect significant drug versus placebo differences ( 46 , 47 ). In this regard, it must be noted that a significant proportion of our participants had mild depression, as reflected by the mean baseline HAMD-17 score of 19.1. This is important because severity of depressive symptoms is a key moderator of antidepressant efficacy in RCTs, and most contemporary studies of antidepressants performed for regulatory purposes exclude individuals with mild symptom severity to enhance signal detection ( 47 ). For example, Kirsch et al. ’s ( 48 ) meta-analysis of industry-sponsored RCTs of depressive disorders showed that the magnitude of drug versus placebo differences was strongly dependent on the studies’ mean symptom severity, such that studies in which participants’ mean HAMD-17 scores were <25 often failed to detect significant effects favoring the active drug. Similarly, in Fournier et al. ’s ( 49 ) meta-analysis of individual data from investigator-initiated RCTs, patients with pretreatment HAMD-17 scores <20 showed little separation between drug and placebo. The specific effectiveness of escitalopram might also have been attenuated by prescription of lower than ideal doses (i.e., fewer than one-third of participants in the escitalopram group received 20 mg/day, which is a more effective dose of this medication in studies of MDD). It should be kept in mind that all participants in this study received concomitant psychotherapy. Thus, the placebo group was actually a group of people treated with CCBT who also saw a psychiatrist and received a pill placebo. In one meta-analysis using individual patient data, the added value of antidepressant medication—when combined with a focused psychotherapy—was only large among patients with more severe depressive episodes ( 50 ). It was also noteworthy that participants showed little change in immune markers across 10 weeks of treatment, despite experiencing a significant reduction in depressive symptoms. While this observation could reflect the relatively milder nature of the depressive disorders experienced by many participants, it could also reflect the fact that the study was limited to people who had achieved and maintained viral suppression as a result of ART. Although speculative, it is plausible that a study that included more participants with severe depressive symptoms as well as participants with higher HIV viral loads might have resulted in a more informative test of the study’s hypotheses. Conversely, our study might have been unnecessarily exclusive with respect to age and history of how HIV was acquired. For example, we excluded adolescents under the age of 18 and adults older than age 70. We also excluded adults who acquired HIV in utero. Although our intent was to reduce heterogeneity, the resulting study group is less representative of the larger population of treatment-seeking PWH with depression. Another limitation is that we did not enroll matched control groups, which would have permitted the immunologic findings to be placed in a more contemporaneous context. That said, it is not clear whether the comparison group should have consisted of age- and sex-matched healthy participants or PWH with no history of depression. PWH have higher levels of plasma markers of inflammation ( 51 ). The same markers are further increased in PWH and depressive symptoms ( 13 , 22 , 52 , 53 ). A number of studies have observed downregulation of cytokines and other inflammatory factor levels during antidepressant treatment of depression; however, most studies did not enroll PWH. Several studies have demonstrated a beneficial effect of SSRIs on inflammation in depression ( 54 , 55 , 56 , 57 , 58 , 59 , 60 , 61 , 62 ). For example, it was demonstrated that treatment of depression with SSRIs was associated with a decrease in IL-6 ( 57 , 58 , 63 , 64 , 65 , 66 ) and CRP levels ( 63 ). Even though other antidepressants were effective in reducing depression, they were not effective in decreasing cytokine levels ( 67 ). Therefore, we anticipated that SSRIs might have dual immune benefits in depressed people with HIV/AIDS, enhancing innate immunity and decreasing chronic immune activation, both of which are implicated in viral control and HIV disease progression. In this regard, it is relevant that a meta-analysis of 45 studies representing 1517 patients with MDD revealed that antidepressant treatment significantly decreases peripheral levels of IL-6, tumor necrosis factor α (TNF-α), IL-10, and CCL2, but these changes were not associated with treatment response ( 68 ). Several previous studies reported that patients who had not responded to multiple courses of antidepressant medication showed elevated levels of proinflammatory markers. For example, TNF-α and IL-6 levels were found to be elevated in patients with depression and correlated with failed treatment ( 69 ). Another study found that patients with depression with CRP ≥0.8 mg/L had a lower depression remission rate during SSRI treatment ( 70 ). With these issues in mind, our study recruited participants who were not currently taking antidepressants. Although some had prior history of antidepressant treatment, prospective participants with more extensive prior treatment histories were excluded. Exclusion of patients with more treatment-resistant depressive disorders might have affected our results ( 71 ). However, in one earlier study of treatment-naïve patients, response to SSRIs and serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors was associated with significant changes in selected immunologic markers ( 72 ). Another factor that might have adversely affected antidepressant efficacy is nonadherence to pharmacotherapy. However, participants in both arms experienced significant improvement in depression symptoms, which were not associated with changes in immune markers. It is plausible that provision of CBT to all participants confounded interpretation of the effects of active escitalopram versus placebo. A number of previous studies have examined the impact of CBT on inflammatory markers in people with depression. The most comprehensive meta-analysis of relevant studies found significant reduction of at least 1 inflammatory marker in 14 of 23 studies ( 73 ). However, 6 of the studies reported increases in at least 1 proinflammatory marker following CBT, and 6 studies found no changes in any immunologic measure. Another recent meta-analysis addressed the impact of CBT on IL-6 levels in patients with depression ( 74 ). In 5 of 10 studies, CBT was associated with lowered IL-6 levels, while no changes were observed in 5 studies. Our study did not detect evidence that treatment of depression with CCBT—with or without active escitalopram—significantly decreased inflammation (IL-6, CRP) or restored immune function (LUNKs, icIFN-γ) in PWH. It is possible that the impact of ART in our sample of PWH, which led to viral suppression and arrested disease progression, had an unanticipated effect that dampened our ability to observe immunologic changes associated with the reduction of depressive symptoms that accompanied treatment with CCBT and either placebo or escitalopram. It may be that our study group of PWH, which was characterized by mild to moderate levels of depressive symptoms and viral suppression on ART, had too-low levels of inflammatory markers to show state-dependent changes following treatment. As noted above, not including comparison groups in our study, whether healthy or HIV-infected control groups, limits the interpretability of our findings. However, we can use CRP levels as one relatively well-studied index of inflammatory activity. A meta-analysis of 64 published studies of plasma CRP concentration in participants with MDD suggests a modest elevation similar to our patient group ( 75 ). Another recent review of 56 studies of people with depressive symptoms reported an average CRP concentration of 0.43 mg/L (95% CI, 0.16–0.72), which was higher than that observed in healthy control individuals ( 76 ). In contrast, for our sample, the CRP distribution percentiles at baseline were as follows: 25th percentile = 0.92, 50th percentile = 2.67, 90th percentile = 11.52, and 99th percentile = 38.92. By comparison, CRP levels in healthy young adult volunteer blood donors are substantially lower, with a median CRP concentration of 0.8 mg/L, a 90th percentile of 3.0 mg/L and a 99th percentile of 10 mg/L ( 77 ). Finally, although our study is to our knowledge the largest placebo-controlled trial of the impact of antidepressant therapy on immune function in PWH, enrollment was adversely affected by the COVID-19 pandemic and, after 5 years of recruitment, we were only able to recruit about two-thirds of the projected sample size (i.e., 110 vs. 180). This unfortunate reality increases the possibility of false negative findings (type II error). However, only one of the observed outcomes would have reached conventional statistical significance if the study had enrolled 180 participants instead of 110, namely the change in CRP levels from pre- to posttreatment pooling across the 2 treatment conditions. Conclusions A carefully screened group of PWH taking ART and who currently had mild to moderate depression experienced a clinically meaningful reduction in depressive symptoms during treatment with CCBT and either active escitalopram or placebo. However, we did not find evidence that either 1) receiving active escitalopram (vs. placebo) or 2) the magnitude of improvement in depressive symptoms significantly improved innate immunity or reduced systemic inflammation. It is conceivable that pretreatment inflammation, as approximated by baseline CRP levels, is driven by both severity of depression and activity of HIV infection. If so, the level persistent inflammation in a group of patients with relatively mild depressive disorder and complete suppression of viral activity may be relatively resistant to a 10-week course of antidepressant medication and CCBT. Conversely, it is conceivable that large treatment effects on innate immunity or indices of inflammation may only be observed in PWH experiencing more severe depressive symptoms and/or receiving less vigorous antiviral treatment.

[conclusion] Conclusions A carefully screened group of PWH taking ART and who currently had mild to moderate depression experienced a clinically meaningful reduction in depressive symptoms during treatment with CCBT and either active escitalopram or placebo. However, we did not find evidence that either 1) receiving active escitalopram (vs. placebo) or 2) the magnitude of improvement in depressive symptoms significantly improved innate immunity or reduced systemic inflammation. It is conceivable that pretreatment inflammation, as approximated by baseline CRP levels, is driven by both severity of depression and activity of HIV infection. If so, the level persistent inflammation in a group of patients with relatively mild depressive disorder and complete suppression of viral activity may be relatively resistant to a 10-week course of antidepressant medication and CCBT. Conversely, it is conceivable that large treatment effects on innate immunity or indices of inflammation may only be observed in PWH experiencing more severe depressive symptoms and/or receiving less vigorous antiviral treatment.

retrieved_papers.title: Dietary salt, intracellular ion homeostasis and hypertension secondary to early-stage kidney disease.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 1996]
[Abstract] Abnormal salt metabolism plays a central role in the pathogenesis of hypertension caused by early-stage renal disease. The mechanisms by which alterations in salt balance and the vasoconstrictor state are related are unclear. We studied the effects of three different salt diets (3, 6-9 and 12 g sodium chloride) on cellular ion homeostasis in platelets in 20 patients with renal hypertension. Compared to normal subjects, patients with renal hypertension had raised cytosolic sodium and calcium concentrations. The platelet sodium pump activity was significantly depressed and calcium ATPase activity increased. These abnormalities of cellular ion metabolism were aggravated by salt overload. On the other hand, dietary salt restriction tended to normalise these parameters. The data presented indicate that a circulating inhibitor of the sodium pump plays a central role in the pathogenesis of salt-induced renal hypertension and that the final common pathway of hypertension secondary to early-stage parenchymal renal disease is an elevation of resting cytosolic calcium followed by an elevation of peripheral vascular resistance.

[Introduction] Background Chronic kidney disease (CKD) is characterized by hypertension, which is thought to be predominately salt sensitive [ 1 ]. High salt intake in CKD patients may cause high blood pressure, increased albuminuria and increased filtration fraction [ 2 - 7 ]. Salt restriction can result in diminished albuminuria and preservation of renal function [ 8 - 10 ]. The INTERSALT study [ 11 ] estimated salt intake by 24 h urine collection at 52 centers in 32 countries, and showed that the mean daily salt intake was 9.2 g. However, the average estimated salt intake by 24 h urine collection obtained from 3 Japanese centers was 11.0 g, suggesting that the Japanese have a relatively higher than average salt intake. The major methods in current use for assessment salt intake are dietary recall and 24 h urine sodium excretion measurement. Dietary recall is often inaccurate, and many patients are truly unaware of the amount of sodium they consume. Measurement of 24 h urine sodium excretion is considered to be reliable, and has been used in many clinical and epidemiological studies, including the INTERSALT study. However, it is relatively difficult to perform because of the necessity of 24 h urine sampling, and inadequate urine pooling leads to under- or over-estimation of salt intake. The use of a spot urine method, or a brief timed collection, for assessing sodium excretion has previously been examined [ 12 - 14 ]. Tanaka et al. [ 12 ] developed a simple method to estimate 24 h urinary sodium excretion from spot urine specimens collected at any time, using 591 Japanese data items from the INTERSALT study. Estimated sodium excretion was significantly correlated with measured sodium excretion using 24 h urine collection among these populations. There have been few reports about estimated salt intake in patients with a decline in renal function. The purpose of the present study was to evaluate whether the estimation of salt intake from spot urine could predict real salt intake in patients with CKD.

[introduction] Background Chronic kidney disease (CKD) is characterized by hypertension, which is thought to be predominately salt sensitive [ 1 ]. High salt intake in CKD patients may cause high blood pressure, increased albuminuria and increased filtration fraction [ 2 - 7 ]. Salt restriction can result in diminished albuminuria and preservation of renal function [ 8 - 10 ]. The INTERSALT study [ 11 ] estimated salt intake by 24 h urine collection at 52 centers in 32 countries, and showed that the mean daily salt intake was 9.2 g. However, the average estimated salt intake by 24 h urine collection obtained from 3 Japanese centers was 11.0 g, suggesting that the Japanese have a relatively higher than average salt intake. The major methods in current use for assessment salt intake are dietary recall and 24 h urine sodium excretion measurement. Dietary recall is often inaccurate, and many patients are truly unaware of the amount of sodium they consume. Measurement of 24 h urine sodium excretion is considered to be reliable, and has been used in many clinical and epidemiological studies, including the INTERSALT study. However, it is relatively difficult to perform because of the necessity of 24 h urine sampling, and inadequate urine pooling leads to under- or over-estimation of salt intake. The use of a spot urine method, or a brief timed collection, for assessing sodium excretion has previously been examined [ 12 - 14 ]. Tanaka et al. [ 12 ] developed a simple method to estimate 24 h urinary sodium excretion from spot urine specimens collected at any time, using 591 Japanese data items from the INTERSALT study. Estimated sodium excretion was significantly correlated with measured sodium excretion using 24 h urine collection among these populations. There have been few reports about estimated salt intake in patients with a decline in renal function. The purpose of the present study was to evaluate whether the estimation of salt intake from spot urine could predict real salt intake in patients with CKD.

[methods] Methods A total of 100 CKD subjects were recruited from the outpatient department of The Jikei University Kashiwa Hospital between October 2009 and May 2010. All patients were requested to collect urine for 24 h. Salt intake was assessed by determining urinary sodium excretion from the collected urine samples. Furthermore, spot urine samples were examined on the same day that patients brought their 24 h urine samples to the hospital. These measurements were performed at least twice during the study period. We also measured urinary creatinine (Cr) excretion using 24 h urine samples.
